name: Camino del Cid: The Three Taifas BTT Route
name_original: Alcoroches y Orihuela del Tremedal desde Checa — circular por el Alto de las Naveras
description: Cycle the difficult Camino del Cid: The Three Taifas BTT Route, a 25-mile journey through historical landscapes and varied terrain.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Checa offers routes through a landscape characterized by mountainous terrain and river valleys. The region features significant elevation changes, with routes often traversing remote areas. This environment provides a challenging backdrop for touring cyclists seeking longer distances and varied gradients. Best touring cycling routes around Checa The most popular touring cycling route is Empty Mountains: Guadalaviar Loop, a 31.6 miles (50.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 32 minutes to complete. This route features notable elevation gain through a remote, scenic landscape. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Empty Mountains Route, a difficult 38.7 miles (62.3 km) path. This trail offers extensive views across open, undeveloped areas with significant climbs. Local touring cyclists also love the Camino del Cid: The Three Taifas BTT Route, a 25.0 miles (40.2 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 41 minutes. Touring cycling around Checa is defined by mountainous landscapes, remote valleys, and challenging ascents. The network offers options for moderate to advanced ability levels, with 5 moderate and 27 difficult routes available. The routes in Checa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 17 reviews. More than 400 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Checa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes around Checa?
A: Checa's touring cycling routes are predominantly challenging, with 27 out of 32 routes rated as difficult. There are also 5 moderate routes available for those seeking a less intense experience. The terrain features significant elevation changes and remote sections.

Q: Are there any beginner-friendly touring cycling routes in Checa?
A: While most routes are difficult, Checa does offer 5 moderate touring cycling routes. These still involve varied terrain and elevation, but are less demanding than the more advanced options. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1190898341/camino-del-cid-the-three-taifas-btt-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cid: The Three Taifas BTT Route&lt;/a&gt;, while rated difficult, is shorter than some other options, covering 40.2 km.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring bike trails in Checa?
A: The touring bike trails in Checa are characterized by mountainous landscapes, remote river valleys, and significant elevation changes. You'll encounter varied gradients and often traverse undeveloped areas, providing a challenging and scenic backdrop for your ride.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Checa?
A: Yes, there are circular touring cycling routes in Checa. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e956564587/empty-mountains-guadalaviar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Empty Mountains: Guadalaviar Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a 50.9 km circuit through a remote and scenic landscape.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or viewpoints along the touring cycling routes?
A: The region around Checa offers stunning natural attractions. You can find impressive gorges like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5070873" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peralejos de las Truchas Cliffs&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5181829" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peralejos de las Truchas Canyon&lt;/a&gt;. Mountain passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5166914" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cubillo Pass&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5181794" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Noguera&lt;/a&gt; also provide expansive views.

Q: What is the best season for touring cycling in Checa?
A: Given the mountainous terrain and potential for remote areas, spring and autumn are generally the most favorable seasons for touring cycling in Checa. Temperatures are milder, and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring challenging conditions in higher elevations.

Q: How long are the touring cycling routes around Checa?
A: The touring cycling routes around Checa vary in length. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086581860/empty-mountains-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Empty Mountains Route&lt;/a&gt; is 62.3 km, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086283639/nacimiento-del-rio-cuervo-circular-desde-tragacete" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nacimiento del río Cuervo — circular desde Tragacete&lt;/a&gt; is 25.9 km. Most routes are designed for longer distances, often taking several hours to complete.

Q: Are there any specific trails that offer extensive views?
A: Yes, many routes in Checa offer extensive views, particularly those traversing open, undeveloped areas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086581860/empty-mountains-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Empty Mountains Route&lt;/a&gt; is known for its extensive views across open landscapes with significant climbs, providing panoramic vistas.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Checa?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 17 reviews. Reviewers often praise the challenging nature of the routes, the remote and scenic landscapes, and the sense of adventure offered by the significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through mountain passes?
A: Yes, several routes in the Checa region incorporate mountain passes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086082079/el-cubillo-el-portillo-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cubillo - El Portillo Trail&lt;/a&gt; features significant elevation gain, likely passing near or over features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5166914" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cubillo Pass&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: Spring drinking fountain at the entrance of Orihuela very rich and fresh to hydrate.
Orihuela del Tremedal has its roots in medieval times. It is believed that the town was founded in the 12th century, during the Reconquista, when Christians recovered territories from the Muslims.
Charming mountain village in the Tierra de Molina region at the foot of Alto de las Naveras. Great place for your adventures.

The Refugio de la Fuente de los Vaqueros boasts a spectacular location, just 2 kilometers away
from Checa, and has a chimney.

Checa is a small mountain town with the Cabrillas river running through it. A beautiful stop on the Camino del Cid.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to cycle the Camino del Cid: The Three Taifas BTT Route?
faqs.answer: This challenging BTT route is generally recommended for around 5 to 6 days of riding. Its length of approximately 300 to 313 kilometers, combined with varied and demanding terrain, means it requires a multi-day commitment.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this BTT route?
faqs.answer: The route features highly varied and often demanding terrain. You'll encounter river plains, fruit orchards, and extensive mountain areas. It predominantly uses dirt tracks, country roads, and minor roads. Expect sections with pine groves, scrubland, river gorges, canyons, and cliffs, especially from Molina de Aragón to Albarracín. Some higher elevation sections may be less well-maintained.

faqs.question: Is the Three Taifas BTT Route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is generally rated as "difficult" and is considered physically demanding, often requiring advanced riding skills. It's one of the more challenging sections of the Camino del Cid, with significant elevation changes and "inhospitable mountain ranges." Beginners may find certain sections with difficult terrain or steep ascents necessitate dismounting and walking.

faqs.question: What historical and natural sights can I expect to see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in history and natural beauty. You'll pass through four important medieval walled towns: Calatayud, Daroca, Molina de Aragón, and Albarracín. Natural highlights include the Natural Reserve of the Upper Tagus, the Mountains of Picaza, the Bogs of Orihuela, the Juniper Forest of Monterde de Albarracín, and the Pine Groves of Ródeno. You can also visit the Fuente Lachmimillo, the charming village of Checa, and the settlement of Orihuela del Tremedal.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder. Winters can be very cold in higher altitude areas like Calamocha, Molina de Aragón, and Albarracín, making cycling challenging. Summers can be very hot, especially in the plains.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Camino del Cid: The Three Taifas BTT Route?
faqs.answer: While there are no explicit restrictions against dogs on the general route, much of it passes through natural areas, including the ZEC Sabinares Rastreros de Alustante-Tordesilos. It's always best to keep dogs on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure their safety, especially given the varied terrain and potential for encountering other cyclists or livestock.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are required to cycle the Camino del Cid: The Three Taifas BTT Route itself. However, parts of the route traverse protected natural areas like the ZEC Sabinares Rastreros de Alustante-Tordesilos. Always respect local regulations regarding nature conservation, waste disposal, and fire prevention within these zones.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access the trailhead for this route?
faqs.answer: The route is a section of the larger Camino del Cid, covering approximately 300-313 km across Zaragoza, Guadalajara, and Teruel. As it's a linear route, you'll need to plan your start and end points. Many cyclists begin in towns like Calatayud or Daroca. Parking is generally available in the larger towns along the route, such as Alcoroches or Orihuela del Tremedal, but specific trailhead parking can vary. Consider public transport options to return to your starting point if you're not doing a full loop.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route overlaps with several other trails. Notably, 44% of it follows the GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT, and 40% is part of Las Tres Taifas. You'll also find sections that coincide with the Camino del Cid (Senderista) and Montañas Vacías.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the route?
faqs.answer: The diverse ecosystems along the route, including pine and juniper forests, are home to various wildlife. You might observe birds of prey, deer, and mountain goats, especially in the wilder mountain sections and natural reserves.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenging sections or insider tips for this route?
faqs.answer: Be prepared for two particularly steep climbs, including the Alto de Chequilla, which will test your endurance. While the route is well-signposted, it's advisable to carry detailed maps or a GPS device, especially in less populated areas. The route often provides BTT-specific alternatives when the hiking path becomes too technical, so keep an eye out for these to ensure a smoother ride.
